region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a chapati entry but does not have a specific combined chapati-with-mutton-keema entry; the chapati node is used as the closest defensible anchor. The mutton keema component is not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers chapati/roti as an unleavened flatbread. No combined chapati-with-keema composite code exists in FoodEx2; the flatbread anchor is used as the primary node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chapati with mutton keema as a composite dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular composite South Asian dish codes.

serving.description: 1 chapati with keema (approximately 1 chapati + 2–3 tablespoons keema)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of one chapati (approximately 60–70 g) paired with a portion of mutton keema (approximately 80–90 g), totalling around 150 g per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati with Mutton / Keema — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per-100g nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Chapati W Mutton / Keema' (cr_id G00010877) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB record and has been estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable whole-wheat chapati with minced lamb keema; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for chapati (whole wheat)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for the composite dish 'chapati with mutton keema'. The GI of 52 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole-wheat chapati (glucose reference = 100). The addition of mutton keema (protein and fat) further blunts the glycaemic response; the composite GI is therefore conservatively estimated at 52 or lower. GL per serving = GI × (carbohydrate_g per serving / 100) = 52 × (43.1 × 1.5 / 100) ≈ 52 × 0.6465 ≈ 16.9 (based on 150 g serving providing ~64.65 g carbohydrate). Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 430 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; potassium at 250 mg/100 g and dietary fibre at 6.0 g/100 g provide partial offset but are insufficient to fully counterbalance the sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to one chapati with a small keema serving and request reduced-salt preparation to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Whole-wheat chapati provides 6.0 g dietary fibre/100 g which moderates glucose absorption; 12.3 g protein from mutton keema further blunts the glycaemic response; estimated GI of 52 (low category) and sugar of 4.41 g/100 g are favourable.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes may include this dish in a balanced meal plan by limiting to one chapati per serving and pairing with non-starchy vegetables to manage total carbohydrate load and maintain postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is low at 3.55 g/100 g; however, mutton is a source of saturated fat and arachidonic acid (omega-6), and sodium at 430 mg/100 g is a concern for blood pressure management relevant to CVD risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should choose lean mutton keema preparations with minimal added oil, limit portion size to control sodium and saturated fat intake, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to support lipid and blood pressure management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokine pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; spices in the keema provide partial anti-inflammatory benefit but do not offset the pro-inflammatory red meat component.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting mutton with chicken, fish, or legumes to reduce arachidonic acid load; if consuming mutton keema, limit frequency and portion size, and note that tomato (a nightshade) may trigger symptom sensitivity in some individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapati with Mutton Keema is a popular South Asian composite dish widely consumed in Singapore, particularly within the Indian community. It consists of a whole-wheat unleavened flatbread (chapati) served alongside or stuffed with spiced minced mutton (keema). The chapati is made from atta (whole-wheat flour), water, and a small amount of oil, rolled thin and dry-roasted on a tawa (griddle) until lightly charred. The mutton keema is prepared by sautéing minced mutton with onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, and a blend of spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, and chilli, producing a fragrant, moist, and mildly spiced meat filling. The texture of the chapati is soft and slightly chewy with a mild nutty flavour from the whole wheat, while the keema is savoury, aromatic, and protein-rich. Nutritionally, the dish provides 254 kcal/100 g with 12.3 g protein, 3.55 g fat, and 43.1 g carbohydrate. The high dietary fibre content of 6.0 g/100 g from whole-wheat flour moderates glucose absorption, making the estimated glycaemic index of 52 (low category) clinically relevant for blood glucose management. Sodium at 430 mg/100 g is a moderate concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker and restaurant contexts. It is commonly served at lunch and dinner and is a staple in Indian Muslim and South Indian eateries across Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is chapati with mutton keema healthy?
How many calories in chapati with mutton keema?

Can I eat chapati keema if I have high blood pressure?

How much protein in chapati mutton keema?
Is chapati mutton keema suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010877) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central for a comparable preparation and is documented. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole-wheat chapati as the closest approved analogous food.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for whole-wheat chapati with minced lamb keema (closest approved-source analogue); this value is not reported in the HPB source record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record; this is confirmed zero per the HPB database entry and is retained as reported. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on whole-wheat chapati from the University of Sydney GI Database; the protein and fat content of the mutton keema component would further reduce the effective glycaemic response of the composite dish. 5. The dish is classified as halal based on the use of mutton and standard Indian spice ingredients without pork or alcohol; individual preparation contexts should be verified.
